content:
# Romans Chapter 7 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_007_page_001.jpg

1. Or do you not know, brothers — for I am speaking to those who know the law — that the law is binding on a person only as long as he lives?

2. For a married woman is bound by law to her husband while he lives, but if her husband dies she is released from the law of marriage.

3. Accordingly, she will be called an adulteress if she lives with another man while her husband is alive. But if her husband dies, she is free from that law, and if she marries another man she is not an adulteress.

4. Likewise, my brothers, you also have died to the law through the body of Christ, so that you may belong to another, to him who has been raised from the dead, in order that we may bear fruit for God.

5. For while we were living in the flesh, our sinful passions, aroused by the law, were at work in our members to bear fruit for death.

6. But now we are released from the law, having died to that which held us captive, so that we serve in the new way of the Spirit and not in the old way of the written code.

7. What then shall we say? That the law is sin? By no means! Yet if it had not been for the law, I would not have known sin. For I would not have known what it is to covet if the law had not said, "You shall not covet."

8. But sin, seizing an opportunity through the commandment, produced in me all kinds of covetousness. For apart from the law, sin lies dead.

9. I was once alive apart from the law, but when the commandment came, sin came alive and I died.

10. The very commandment that promised life proved to be death to me.

11. For sin, seizing an opportunity through the commandment, deceived me and through it killed me.

12. So the law is holy, and the commandment is holy and righteous and good.

13. Did that which is good, then, bring death to me? By no means! It was sin, producing death in me through what is good, in order that sin might be shown to be sin, and through the commandment might become sinful beyond measure.

14. For we know that the law is spiritual, but I am of the flesh, sold under sin.

15. For I do not understand my own actions. For I do not do what I want, but I do the very thing I hate.

16. Now if I do what I do not want, I agree with the law, that it is good.

17. So now it is no longer I who do it, but sin that dwells within me.

18. For I know that nothing good dwells in me, that is, in my flesh. For I have the desire to do what is right, but not the ability to carry it out.

19. For I do not do the good I want, but the evil I do not want is what I keep on doing.

20. Now if I do what I do not want, it is no longer I who do it, but sin that dwells within me.

21. So I find it to be a law that when I want to do right, evil lies close at hand.

22. For I delight in the law of God, in my inner being,

23. but I see in my members another law waging war against the law of my mind and making me captive to the law of sin that dwells in my members.

24. Wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?

25. Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, I myself serve the law of God with my mind, but with my flesh I serve the law of sin.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Romans 7 is Paul's most psychologically intense chapter — a passage that has shaped Western understanding of human inner conflict from Augustine through Luther to Freud. It defends the law while exposing its limitation: the law can diagnose but cannot cure.

**Verses 7-12 — The law vindicated:** EMET notes Paul's choice of the tenth commandment (coveting), the most internal command. PNEUMA captures the dynamic: "sin as parasite" — it hijacks God's good command. SOPHIA affirms: Paul's argument is precise. The law is not sin (v.7); the law is holy (v.12). Sin is the agent of death, not the law. The law is like a diagnostic tool — an X-ray reveals the tumor but doesn't cause it. Sin uses the commandment as a "beachhead" (ἀφορμή) — a military term for a base of operations. The fault lies with the invader, not the territory.

**Verses 14-25 — The identity of the "I":** EMET surveys the options (autobiographical, Adamic, Israel, humanity). LOGOS notes the tense shift (past in vv.7-13, present in vv.14-25). SOPHIA's adjudication: the "I" is multivalent. Paul uses first person singular to describe a universal human experience — the divided self that knows the good but cannot do it. Whether this describes the pre-Christian under law (Eastern Fathers) or the ongoing Christian struggle (Western tradition, especially Augustine and Luther), the phenomenological description rings true across both experiences. The resolution is not found within the self but in Christ (v.25) and the Spirit (ch.8).

**Verse 18 — "Nothing good dwells in me, that is, in my flesh":** PNEUMA rightly notes the qualifying clause. Paul is not teaching total depravity in the sense of absolute worthlessness. The "flesh" (σάρξ) is the unredeemed dimension of human existence — the self oriented away from God. The inner being delights in God's law (v.22), but the flesh is a occupied territory. The will is intact; the power is absent.

**Verse 24 — The cry of humanity:** LOGOS identifies the arc: analysis → agony → doxology. SOPHIA affirms: "Who will deliver me?" is the essential human question. Notice: it is "who," not "what." The answer is not a method, a technique, or more effort. It is a person — "Jesus Christ our Lord." And the mechanism of that deliverance — the Spirit — awaits in chapter 8, which begins with "no condemnation" and ends with "nothing can separate us." Chapter 7 is the darkness before the dawn.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
